title: AFRIKANER Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a special way for white South Africans to come to America as refugees if they are being hurt or threatened because of their race. It lets them and their families apply to live in the United States without having to compete for the usual limited number of refugee spots. The government would have to write reports about how many people apply and what happens to them.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Asylum for Farmers and Refugees In Crisis And Necessary Emigration Resettlement Act or the AFRIKANER Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Designation of Certain Residents of South Africa as Priority 2 Refugees
sections.text: The Secretary of State working with the Secretary of Homeland Security must give special refugee status called Priority 2 refugees of special humanitarian concern to certain people. This status goes to people from South Africa who are white and have been hurt or have good reason to fear being hurt because of their race, ethnic background, or family heritage. It also includes their spouses, children, and parents as long as those parents are from South Africa and not citizens of another country. These people can apply for refugee status while they are still in South Africa or in another country. A person cannot be denied the chance to apply for refugee status just because they have family who are American citizens or because they could get into America through other immigration programs. People from South Africa cannot be denied refugee status mainly because they were arrested, held in jail, or faced other government actions against them for political reasons related to their race, ethnic background, or family heritage. People who get refugee status under this law do not count against the normal limits on how many refugees America accepts each year. The Secretary of State and Secretary of Homeland Security must write reports every ninety days starting one hundred eighty days after this law begins. These reports go to certain committees in Congress and must include how many applications are waiting to be processed, how long people wait for different steps like job checks and interviews, and how many applications were denied and why. These reports must be shared with the public on the State Department website and can be unclassified but may have secret parts. People who get Priority 2 refugee status under this law are considered to meet all the requirements needed to enter America as refugees.
